agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands courier location never be shown due to driver retention problems. This is a fundamental business conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level — it requires executive alignment on company strategy.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A driver or delivery person who physically transports a package from origin to destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to the customer with their package."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is exposed — ranges from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation (e.g., '2 stops away')."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The state between pickup confirmation and delivery confirmation, during which tracking could theoretically be shown."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The ability to keep couriers on the platform; a critical supply-side metric in courier logistics."},{"term":"Customer transparency","definition":"The degree to which order status and courier progress are visible to the end customer."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — depends on which stakeholder position prevails. Exact GPS makes stationarity visible and may generate complaint calls. Abstracted tracking could mask this."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop optimization)","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — exact GPS would expose routing logic and multi-drop behavior to customers, potentially causing confusion or complaints. Operations would cite this as another reason to restrict visibility."},{"scenario":"Courier is in a sensitive location (hospital, government building)","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — privacy and safety implications of exposing exact location in sensitive areas are unresolved."},{"scenario":"Customer harasses or stalks courier using real-time location","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — this is a driver safety concern that supports Operations' position but is unaddressed by Customer Success requirements."},{"scenario":"Courier ends shift or reassignment mid-delivery","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — showing exact GPS during handoff could expose operational complexity to customers."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH STAKES BOTH DIRECTIONS. Customer Success argues transparency drives conversion and retention (revenue). Operations argues driver churn costs $3K-$5K per replacement in recruiting, onboarding, and lost capacity (cost). Both have legitimate financial claims. Without resolution, either path carries material financial risk.","operational":"Showing exact GPS increases support call volume (customers calling about stationary couriers, route deviations). NOT showing location may also increase support calls ('where is my package?'). Driver retention directly impacts delivery capacity and SLA compliance. A middle-ground (abstracted progress) could optimize both, but neither team has been asked to consider compromise.","user":"Two distinct user populations with opposing needs. CUSTOMERS want predictability and control — they want to know when to be available. COURIERS want dignity and autonomy — constant surveillance degrades job satisfaction. Both are 'users' of the platform. Privileging one over the other is a strategic choice, not a technical one.","risk":"LEGAL/REGULATORY: Real-time GPS tracking of workers raises labor law and privacy regulation concerns in many jurisdictions (GDPR, state privacy laws). SAFETY: Exposing exact courier location creates physical safety risks (stalking, robbery, confrontation). RETENTION: Driver shortage is an industry-wide crisis — policies that accelerate churn create existential supply-side risk. COMPETITIVE: Lack of any tracking may put the platform behind competitors who offer it.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — their ability to track deliveries determines experience quality, anxiety level, and willingness to be available for receipt."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — their sense of autonomy, safety, and dignity at work. Operations reports this is already causing attrition."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Their KPIs (NPS, CSAT, churn) are tied to transparency features they have promised or marketed."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Their KPIs (driver retention, delivery capacity, cost-per-delivery) are degraded by surveillance-style tracking."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — real-time worker GPS tracking has significant legal implications that neither team has addressed."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict — it is a strategic priority decision, not a feature decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because the specification contains a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams. Both positions are presented as non-negotiable, making it impossible to define correct behavior. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in on what may be a regulated decision. No acceptance criteria can be written until leadership resolves the strategic conflict. The confidence level reflects spec quality, not analysis quality — and this spec is fundamentally broken.","high_confidence_areas":["Both stakeholder concerns are legitimate and well-understood in the logistics industry","There ARE middle-ground solutions used across the industry (abstracted progress indicators, ETA-based tracking, zone-level visibility) that could satisfy both parties","Driver safety and privacy are real, material concerns that cannot be dismissed","Customer desire for delivery predictability is real and commercially important"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder priority the business will choose to optimize for","Whether Legal/Compliance constraints further restrict the option space","Whether either team would accept a compromise position","The actual magnitude of driver attrition attributable specifically to location tracking vs. other factors"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholder concerns","No executive decision has already been made that supersedes these requirements","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker location tracking is legally constrained"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires courier GPS coordinates to be shown to customers at all times during delivery. Operations requires courier location to NEVER be shown to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from teams with equal authority. This cannot be resolved by engineering — it is a strategic business decision that requires executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — risk: accelerated driver churn)","B: No location visibility (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — risk: competitive disadvantage, higher support volume)","C: Abstracted progress tracking — show ETA, delivery stage, or zone-level proximity without exact GPS (industry-standard compromise used by UPS, FedEx, Amazon). Requires BOTH teams to negotiate.","D: Courier opt-in — let drivers choose their visibility level, shifting control to the courier (preserves autonomy, but creates inconsistent customer experience)"],"recommendation":"Option C — abstracted progress tracking. This is the proven industry pattern. Customers get predictability (ETA, '2 stops away', 'arriving soon') without exact GPS. Couriers are not surveilled. However, this recommendation CANNOT be implemented until executive leadership formally arbitrates the conflict and both teams agree to the compromise. Recommend an immediate stakeholder alignment meeting with VP-level decision authority before any engineering work begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and Compliance review has not been conducted. Real-time GPS tracking of workers is regulated under GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), and various labor laws. In some jurisdictions, continuous location monitoring of employees requires explicit consent, data minimization, and purpose limitation. This legal review could eliminate Option A entirely.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers/Drivers","Executive leadership"],"options":["Engage Legal/Compliance for a privacy and labor law review before proceeding","Proceed without legal review (NOT recommended)"],"recommendation":"Legal review must happen before any technical design. If exact GPS tracking is legally constrained, the decision space narrows significantly and may resolve the stakeholder conflict by elimination."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot proceed to implementation — specification contains a direct contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams.","business_rule_broken":"No business rules can be defined when the fundamental requirement (show vs. hide courier location) is contested by stakeholders with equal authority."},{"violation":"Missing Legal/Compliance review for worker location tracking.","business_rule_broken":"Privacy and labor law constraints have not been evaluated, meaning any implementation could violate regulatory requirements."}]}
